content.full_text:
State of California
GOVERNOR'S OFFICE
SACRAMENTO, CA 95814

August 26, 1991

PETE WILSON
GOVERNOR

TELEPHONE
(916) 445-2841

Mark T. Morodomi, Esq.
Legal Division
Fair Political Practices Commission
P. O. Box 807
Sacramento, California 95804-0807

Dear Mr. Morodomi:

We have reviewed the proposed regulations governing gifts
under Government Code section 89504 et seq. On the whole they
appear quite clear and cover the subject area. However, there are
a several areas that are problematic for the Governor. It is
possible that additional exemptions or regulations could be
formulated to address these concerns, if such exclusions or
regulations are consistent with underlying statutes. In the
alternative, we need informal advice in the following areas.

First, there is a problem with reporting tickets or passes
that have been given to the Governor himself, not his office. In
allowing disqualifications, the regulations seem limited to
occasions when the gift could potentially influence a governmental
decision. In contrast, the Governor is given many complimentary
tickets or passes that have no connection with any particular
governmental decision.

In fact, these are courtesy tickets or passes given to the
Governor simply because he is the Governor, by the entity
operating the event or business. These tickets or passes are
generally nontransferable and regarded by the companies as part of
their public relations budget. If the Governor did use the
tickets, it would be a media coup. In light of this, there is
some feeling that returning the tickets would be an insult. In
most cases, these tickets or passes do not make it to the
Governor's attention and are never used. Further, given the
volume of mail and the fact that all mail is read and then routed
to an appropriate person for response, by the time the tickets or
passes are brought to the attention of Scheduling or Legal
Affairs, the 30 day time limit for return has expired.

The Governor is in a unique position and the regulations do
not clearly address this issue. For the record, any tickets or
passes that are used are reported. The question remains: can all
of these tickets and passes be disqualified if they are not used?

Mark T. Morodomi, Esq.
August 26, 1991
Page Two


     The second question is: how does this office go about
assigning a "fair market value" to gifts that defy evaluating?
For example, the German ambassador presented Governor Wilson with
a piece of the Berlin Wall. This is a gift of history for which
it is difficult, if not impossible, to ascertain a monetary value.
There are other gifts of this kind, and, I am sure, these will not
be the last. The Governor of California is probably more likely
to receive gifts of this nature than other State officials.

     Additionally, I would like to confirm your oral advice
concerning the regular use of typical California products. You
agreed that a public official could solicit fruit, produce, or
other California grown or produced products to be served as
refreshments or given as gifts to visiting foreign dignitaries to
promote California business and trade. These products are not for
personal use or gain, but to showcase California products and
induce foreign interests to trade or otherwise do business with
California.

     Like some of the gifts mentioned above, this is a unique
situation in which public officials would have an opportunity to
promote business and increase revenues for California. You agreed
these items would not need to be reported as gifts.

     As you can see, regulations which address the unique issues,
as well as the routine would be very helpful. Even though this
office probably encounters the unique more often, I am sure other
officials must be faced with the same dilemmas. For this reason,
we are suggesting that regulations might be formulated to address
these concerns.

     If you do not feel that regulations are appropriate, this
office would appreciate an informal opinion to guide our staff.
Thank you for taking the time to consider our concerns. If you
have any questions, feel free to contact me.

     As a footnote, there is one other portion of the regulations,
2 CCR § 18945 et seq. that we will be addressing in a separate
letter. These provisions affect a larger number of State
officials.

                                        Sincerely,


                                        [signature]

                                        Janice Rogers Brown
                                        Legal Affairs Secretary

sections.question_synthetic: The Governor's office seeks informal FPPC advice on three gift-related issues: (1) whether complimentary tickets/passes given to the Governor that are not used can be disqualified from reporting requirements; (2) how to assign fair market value to unique or historically significant gifts with no clear monetary valuation; and (3) confirmation that California products solicited and given to foreign dignitaries to promote California trade do not require reporting as gifts.
sections.parsing_notes: No section headers found; LLM: This is an incoming request letter from the Governor's office to FPPC, not an FPPC response. No formal QUESTION or CONCLUSION sections present. Document raises three distinct policy questions regarding gift regulations under Government Code section 89501 et seq. Sender requests either regulatory amendments or informal advice letter.

embedding.summary: The Governor's Legal Affairs office requests informal FPPC advice on gift reporting and disqualification issues specific to the Governor's office, including problems with unused complimentary tickets, valuation of unique historical gifts, and the treatment of California products given to visiting dignitaries for trade promotion purposes.
